quote: So to be honest, Tracy, I do think that you don't have enough sun for solely for solar to work for you, unfortunately.
stewart_read: The rep attempted exactly one angle — a satellite image check — before issuing a DQ verdict. The codex target is 3+ angles per objection before conceding. No verify items had been completed. No bill had been discussed. No roof orientation, pitch, or age had been asked. The rep's 'to be honest' framing signals genuine intent to help, but the underlying move is a premature close on a negative outcome against a self-motivated buyer.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep saw severe shading and was protecting Tracy's time by being direct. Rejected — the customer immediately proposed an alternative (garage), demonstrating she had more information than the satellite image showed. A strategic rep would have asked before concluding.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the codex's 3+ angles target. Walk through what angles were available before the DQ verdict: (1) ask about roof orientation and pitch on the main house, (2) ask about the garage roof specifically, (3) ask about any south- or west-facing roof surfaces not visible from satellite. The rep had none of this information when they said 'unfortunately.'

quote: I was looking at we have a detached garage. Okay. Is that possible to put it out there at the garage?
stewart_read: Tracy's immediate pivot to the garage is an enthusiasm signal — she is not accepting the DQ, she is problem-solving in real time. A detached garage with a different roof orientation than the main house is a legitimate installation surface. The rep's response was to look it up on Google Maps and issue the same verdict ('kind of the same thing — too much shade') without asking about the garage roof's age, pitch, orientation, or condition.
coaching_implication: This is the moment the call could have turned. The manager should ask the rep: what questions would you ask about a garage roof before concluding it won't work? Roof age, pitch, cardinal orientation, and tree proximity are all askable. The rep skipped all of them and went back to the satellite.

quote: My thinking is when the sun is going down, that it hits that shed right there.
stewart_read: Tracy is describing observed sun behavior on her property — she has watched the sun hit the shed in the afternoon. This is a customer doing the rep's qualification work for them. A rep working 3+ angles would have asked: what time does the sun hit it, how long does it stay, which direction does the shed face? Instead the rep responded with 'Mhmm. Okay.' and then asked for a moment ('oh, shoot. Sorry. Give me one sec.') before returning to the same satellite-based conclusion.
coaching_implication: Tracy's sun observation is the kind of ground-truth data that overrides satellite imagery. The manager should coach the rep to treat customer-reported sun behavior as a qualification signal worth pursuing, not a detail to acknowledge and move past.

quote: Okay. So for ground mounted systems, we don't offer them just because due to trenching and everything involved. They do tend to get super, super expensive.
stewart_read: When Tracy proposed the side of the house, the rep assumed ground mount and then explained Ion's product limitations — pricing rationale, trenching costs, why it's 'not really worth it.' This is closer-tier content delivered by a setter. The rep's job at this moment was to ask whether Tracy meant a roof surface on a side-facing structure, and if the answer was genuinely ground mount, to route to a specialist rather than explain the economics. Reactive read holds: the rep filled a knowledge gap with a product explanation instead of a routing move.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep was being transparent to save Tracy's time. Rejected — the rep had not yet asked whether Tracy meant a roof surface or a ground array, so the explanation was premature regardless of intent.
coaching_implication: The universal escape hatch from the codex applies here: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer.' The manager should drill the rep on the distinction between explaining product limitations (closer-tier) and routing a question to the right person (setter-tier).

quote: If you were open to cutting down some of the I mean, it looks like you would need to remove quite a bit of trees, but if that was a possibility, then maybe we could do something.
stewart_read: The rep's only forward-looking suggestion after three dismissed alternatives is tree removal — a high-friction, high-cost ask that Tracy immediately declined. The rep had not asked about the side structure's roof surface, had not asked about roof orientation on the main house, and had not routed to a specialist. Tree removal as the final offer signals the rep had exhausted their satellite-based toolkit without ever engaging Tracy's ground-truth knowledge of her property.
coaching_implication: Ask the rep: before suggesting tree removal, what questions did you ask Tracy about her property that a satellite image can't answer? The answer should be: roof pitch, cardinal orientation of each structure, which surfaces get direct sun and for how long. None of those were asked.

quote: But as of right now, we're we're not able. Yeah. I don't think that's what she's gonna do.
stewart_read: Tracy's third-person reference ('she') is ambiguous. The most plausible reading is Tracy speaking colloquially about herself — a self-referential deflection common in conversational speech, particularly when declining a suggestion ('I don't think that's what she's gonna do' = 'that's not something I'm going to do'). The co-decision-maker reading (a spouse or adult child on the title) is possible but unsupported by any prior transcript evidence — no second person was mentioned, no title co-owner was referenced. The rep did not probe either way, which is a missed verify item regardless of which reading is correct.
alternative_hypothesis: Co-decision-maker read: 'she' refers to a spouse or adult child who is the actual decision-maker on tree removal. Possible but unsupported — no prior mention of another person on the title. The self-reference reading is at least equally plausible.
coaching_implication: The manager should note that the rep never asked the title co-owner question (verify.title_co_owners). If 'she' is a co-decision-maker, the rep missed a protocol trigger. If 'she' is self-reference, the rep still missed the verify item. Either way, the question was never asked. Drill the rep on completing verify items before reaching a DQ conclusion.

quote: Well, yeah. So I actually work with our design team. I help get the solar designs created for the homes and everything.
stewart_read: The rep skips the codex intro entirely — no company name stated, no Tesla framing, no state mention, no legitimacy anchor. The rep's self-description ('I work with our design team') is a soft legitimacy claim but does not follow the intro template. Tracy's warm opening meant this gap didn't cost the call immediately, but the rep entered the verify section without establishing the foundational legitimacy the intro is designed to provide.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Tracy was already warm and reading about the company, so the rep skipped the intro to match her energy and move quickly. Partially supported — Tracy did say 'I like that' about being quick. But the intro's legitimacy function is not just about warmth; it anchors the company name and referral source for a customer who may not remember which form they filled out.
coaching_implication: The manager should note that the intro was skipped entirely. Even with a warm customer, the company name and referral framing should be stated — it takes 10 seconds and protects against the customer later wondering who they spoke to.

quote: Well, yeah. So I actually work with our design team. I help get the solar designs created for the homes and everything. And so I just have a couple of things that I would like to confirm with you before I get started.
stewart_read: The rep completed zero of the eight verify items before issuing a DQ verdict at 01:11. The codex requires 6-of-8 for a qualified appointment. The rep confirmed the address (one item) and then went directly to satellite imagery. No bill, no utility company, no interest in solar, no roof age, no title co-owners, no prior designs, no residence length — none of these were asked. The DQ verdict was issued on incomplete information.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep identified a hard DQ condition (shading) early and correctly prioritized it before investing time in full verify. Rejected — shading is not a hard DQ in the codex; it is a site-assessment question that requires specialist input, not a satellite-image verdict from a setter.
coaching_implication: This is the structural root of the call's failure. The manager should walk the rep through the verify checklist and ask: at what point in this call did you have enough information to issue a DQ? The answer is: never — because the verify was never completed. Shading concerns route to a specialist, not to a setter's satellite check.
